description.task: Implement a DatabaseConnectionPool class using the Singleton pattern with double-checked locking for thread safety. The pool maintains a fixed number of connections and provides borrowBorrow/return operations.
instructions: getInstance() uses double-checked locking: check instance==null, enter synchronized block, check again, then create
The instance field is declared volatile to prevent instruction reordering
The second call to getInstance() must return the exact same object regardless of the argument
borrowConnection() decrements the available pool and returns the connection string
returnConnection() returns the connection back to the available pool
description.hint: In getInstance(), use: if (instance == null) { synchronized(DatabaseConnectionPool.class) { if (instance == null) { instance = new DatabaseConnectionPool(maxConnections); } } }
description.expectedOutput:
Borrowed: 2
Available: 1
After return - Available: 2
Same instance: true

description.task: Implement a HttpRequest builder using the Builder pattern. An HTTP request has a URL, method, headers, query parameters, and body. The builder should validate mandatory fields and support an immutable request object.
instructions: Builder.header() adds the name-value pair to headers and returns 'this'
Builder.queryParam() adds to queryParams and returns 'this'
build() throws IllegalStateException('URL is required') if url is null or blank
The resulting HttpRequest has immutable collections for headers and queryParams
The Builder uses a fluent API style — each setter returns 'this'
description.hint: header() and queryParam() just call headers.put(name, value) and queryParams.put(name, value) then return this. The build() validation for body is optional per the task description.
description.expectedOutput:
URL: https://api.example.com/users
Method: POST
Headers: 2
Body: {"name": "Alice"}

description.task: Implement a NotificationFactory using the Factory Method pattern. The factory creates different Notification objects (Email, SMS, Push) based on a channel type enum. Each notification type has its own send logic that formats the message differently.
instructions: create() uses a switch or if-else on the Channel enum to return the correct Notification subclass
create() throws IllegalArgumentException('Unknown channel: ' + channel) for any unrecognized channel
SmsNotification truncates messages longer than 160 characters
All three channel implementations must fully implement both interface methods
description.hint: Use switch(channel) { case EMAIL -> return new EmailNotification(); ... } in create(). For PushNotification.send(), return 'PUSH to device/' + recipient + ': ' + message.
description.expectedOutput:
EMAIL to alice@example.com: Welcome!
SMS to +15551234567: Your OTP is 123456
PUSH to device/device-abc: New message
